1→import type { PrismaClient } from "../prisma/generated/client/index.js";
     2→
     3→// ─── Setup ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
     4→
     5→/**
     6→ * Create the FTS5 virtual table if it doesn't exist.
     7→ * The rowid of LogContentFts equals LogContent.id so results can be joined back.
     8→ * Metadata columns (contentType, logEventId, sessionId, timestamp, toolName) are
     9→ * stored but not indexed (UNINDEXED) — they're used for filtering and display.
    10→ */
    11→export async function setupLogContentFts(db: PrismaClient): Promise<void> {
    12→  await db.$executeRawUnsafe(`
    13→    CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS LogContentFts USING fts5(
    14→      text,
    15→      thinking,
    16→      toolResultContent,
    17→      toolInput,
    18→      contentType   UNINDEXED,
    19→      logEventId    UNINDEXED,
    20→      sessionId     UNINDEXED,
    21→      timestamp     UNINDEXED,
    22→      toolName      UNINDEXED
    23→    )
    24→  `);
    25→}
    26→
    27→// ─── Sync ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    28→
    29→/**
    30→ * Insert any LogContent rows not yet present in the FTS index.
    31→ * Safe to call repeatedly — already-indexed rows are skipped via rowid check.
    32→ * Returns the number of rows inserted.
    33→ */
    34→export async function syncLogContentFts(db: PrismaClient): Promise<number> {
    35→  return db.$executeRawUnsafe(`
    36→    INSERT INTO LogContentFts(
    37→      rowid, text, thinking, toolResultContent, toolInput,
    38→      contentType, logEventId, sessionId, timestamp, toolName
    39→    )
    40→    SELECT
    41→      lc.id,
    42→      COALESCE(lc.text,              ''),
    43→      COALESCE(lc.thinking,          ''),
    44→      COALESCE(lc.toolResultContent, ''),
    45→      COALESCE(lc.toolInput,         ''),
    46→      lc.contentType,
    47→      lc.logEventId,
    48→      COALESCE(le.sessionId,  ''),
    49→      COALESCE(le.timestamp,  ''),
    50→      COALESCE(lc.toolName,   '')
    51→    FROM LogContent lc
    52→    LEFT JOIN LogEvent le ON le.id = lc.logEventId
    53→    WHERE lc.id NOT IN (SELECT rowid FROM LogContentFts)
    54→  `);
    55→}
    56→
    57→/**
    58→ * Drop and rebuild the FTS index from scratch.
    59→ * Use this after bulk imports when incremental sync is too slow.
    60→ */
    61→export async function rebuildLogContentFts(db: PrismaClient): Promise<void> {
    62→  await db.$executeRawUnsafe(`DELETE FROM LogContentFts`);
    63→  await syncLogContentFts(db);
    64→}
    65→
    66→// ─── Search ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    67→
    68→export interface LogContentSearchResult {
    69→  logContentId: number;
    70→  logEventId: number;
    71→  sessionId: string;
    72→  timestamp: string | null;
    73→  contentType: string;
    74→  toolName: string | null;
    75→  /** "user" | "assistant" — which side of the conversation this content came from. */
    76→  logEventType: string;
    77→  gitUserName: string | null;
    78→  gitUserEmail: string | null;
    79→  /** FTS5 snippet with «match» markers around matched terms. */
    80→  snippet: string;
    81→  /** BM25 rank (more negative = better match). */
    82→  rank: number;
    83→}
    84→
    85→/**
    86→ * Full-text search across LogContent fields (text, thinking, toolResultContent,
    87→ * toolInput).  Accepts standard FTS5 MATCH syntax:
    88→ *   - bare word:   foo
    89→ *   - phrase:      "foo bar"
    90→ *   - prefix:      foo*
    91→ *   - column:      text:foo
    92→ *   - boolean:     foo AND bar, foo OR bar, foo NOT bar
    93→ *
    94→ * Results are ordered by BM25 relevance (best first).
    95→ */
    96→export async function searchLogContent(
    97→  db: PrismaClient,
    98→  query: string,
    99→  limit = 50,
   100→): Promise<LogContentSearchResult[]> {
   101→  type Row = {
   102→    logContentId: number | bigint;
   103→    logEventId: number | bigint;
   104→    sessionId: string;
   105→    timestamp: string | null;
   106→    contentType: string;
   107→    toolName: string | null;
   108→    logEventType: string;
   109→    gitUserName: string | null;
   110→    gitUserEmail: string | null;
   111→    snippet: string;
   112→    rank: number;
   113→  };
   114→
   115→  // Run FTS match in a subquery first, then join for extra metadata.
   116→  const rows = await db.$queryRawUnsafe<Row[]>(`
   117→    SELECT
   118→      fts.logContentId,
   119→      fts.logEventId,
   120→      fts.sessionId,
   121→      fts.timestamp,
   122→      fts.contentType,
   123→      fts.toolName,
   124→      fts.snippet,
   125→      fts.rank,
   126→      le.type   AS logEventType,
   127→      s.gitUserName,
   128→      s.gitUserEmail
   129→    FROM (
   130→      SELECT
   131→        rowid                                              AS logContentId,
   132→        logEventId,
   133→        sessionId,
   134→        timestamp,
   135→        contentType,
   136→        toolName,
   137→        snippet(LogContentFts, -1, '«', '»', '…', 24)   AS snippet,
   138→        rank
   139→      FROM LogContentFts
   140→      WHERE LogContentFts MATCH ?
   141→      ORDER BY rank
   142→      LIMIT ?
   143→    ) fts
   144→    JOIN LogEvent le ON le.id = fts.logEventId
   145→    LEFT JOIN Session s ON s.sessionId = fts.sessionId
   146→  `, query, limit);
   147→
   148→  return rows.map((r) => ({
   149→    logContentId:  Number(r.logContentId),
   150→    logEventId:    Number(r.logEventId),
   151→    sessionId:     r.sessionId,
   152→    timestamp:     r.timestamp,
   153→    contentType:   r.contentType,
   154→    toolName:      r.toolName || null,
   155→    logEventType:  r.logEventType,
   156→    gitUserName:   r.gitUserName || null,
   157→    gitUserEmail:  r.gitUserEmail || null,
   158→    snippet:       r.snippet,
   159→    rank:          r.rank,
   160→  }));
   161→}
   162→

content.text:
The logic at lines 39-40 already handles this correctly:
- `isHuman` = `logEventType === "user"` **AND** `contentType !== "tool_result"` — excludes tool_results from being shown as human
- `isClaude` = `logEventType === "assistant"` OR `contentType === "thinking"`

But `tool_result` with `logEventType === "user"` would be neither `isHuman` nor `isClaude`. Let me see what renders in that case.

Found it. When `!isClaude` (which includes `tool_result` results), it falls through to the `else` branch and shows `displayName` ("You") with the user avatar. The fix is simple: `tool_result` should always be attributed to Claude.

input.old_string:
const isHuman = r.logEventType === "user" && r.contentType !== "tool_result";
  const isClaude = r.logEventType === "assistant" || r.contentType === "thinking";
input.new_string:
const isHuman = r.logEventType === "user" && r.contentType !== "tool_result";
  const isClaude = r.logEventType === "assistant" || r.contentType === "thinking" || r.contentType === "tool_result";

content.content: The file /Users/stephanostsoucas/gossamer/app/src/renderer/src/screens/Search.tsx has been updated successfully.

toolUseResult.oldString:
const isHuman = r.logEventType === "user" && r.contentType !== "tool_result";
  const isClaude = r.logEventType === "assistant" || r.contentType === "thinking";
toolUseResult.newString:
const isHuman = r.logEventType === "user" && r.contentType !== "tool_result";
  const isClaude = r.logEventType === "assistant" || r.contentType === "thinking" || r.contentType === "tool_result";

lines: function ResultCard({ r, onClick }: { r: SearchResult; onClick: () => void }) {

content.text: `tool_result` is now always attributed to Claude. The `isHuman` guard was already correct (excluding `tool_result`), but `isClaude` wasn't explicitly catching it — so results with `contentType === "tool_result"` fell through to the user avatar/name. Adding `|| r.contentType === "tool_result"` to `isClaude` fixes it.

message.content: Let's add some filters to the search. I want to be able to filter by type: message, tool_call, tool_result, thinking. The UI should let me pick a filter.

content.text: I'll add client-side type filtering with Mantine `Chip.Group`.

     2→

     4→
     5→/**
     6→ * Create the FTS5 virtual table if it doesn't exist.
     7→ * The rowid of LogContentFts equals LogContent.id so results can be joined back.
     8→ * Metadata columns (contentType, logEventId, sessionId, timestamp, toolName) are
     9→ * stored but not indexed (UNINDEXED) — they're used for filtering and display.
    10→ */
    11→export async function setupLogContentFts(db: PrismaClient): Promise<void> {
    12→  await db.$executeRawUnsafe(`
    13→    CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS LogContentFts USING fts5(
    14→      text,
    15→      thinking,
    16→      toolResultContent,
    17→      toolInput,
    18→      contentType   UNINDEXED,
    19→      logEventId    UNINDEXED,
    20→      sessionId     UNINDEXED,
    21→      timestamp     UNINDEXED,
    22→      toolName      UNINDEXED
    23→    )

    28→

    30→ * Insert any LogContent rows not yet present in the FTS index.
    31→ * Safe to call repeatedly — already-indexed rows are skipped via rowid check.
    32→ * Returns the number of rows inserted.

    34→export async function syncLogContentFts(db: PrismaClient): Promise<number> {
    35→  return db.$executeRawUnsafe(`
    36→    INSERT INTO LogContentFts(
    37→      rowid, text, thinking, toolResultContent, toolInput,
    38→      contentType, logEventId, sessionId, timestamp, toolName
    39→    )
    40→    SELECT
    41→      lc.id,
    42→      COALESCE(lc.text,              ''),
    43→      COALESCE(lc.thinking,          ''),
    44→      COALESCE(lc.toolResultContent, ''),
    45→      COALESCE(lc.toolInput,         ''),
    46→      lc.contentType,
    47→      lc.logEventId,
    48→      COALESCE(le.sessionId,  ''),

    51→    FROM LogContent lc

    66→// ─── Search ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    68→export interface LogContentSearchResult {
    69→  logContentId: number;
    70→  logEventId: number;
    71→  sessionId: string;
    72→  timestamp: string | null;
    73→  contentType: string;
    74→  toolName: string | null;

    86→ * Full-text search across LogContent fields (text, thinking, toolResultContent,
    87→ * toolInput).  Accepts standard FTS5 MATCH syntax:
    88→ *   - bare word:   foo
    89→ *   - phrase:      "foo bar"
    90→ *   - prefix:      foo*
    91→ *   - column:      text:foo
    92→ *   - boolean:     foo AND bar, foo OR bar, foo NOT bar

    94→ * Results are ordered by BM25 relevance (best first).

    96→export async function searchLogContent(
    97→  db: PrismaClient,
    98→  query: string,
    99→  limit = 50,
   100→): Promise<LogContentSearchResult[]> {
   101→  type Row = {

   104→    sessionId: string;
   105→    timestamp: string | null;

   114→

   116→  const rows = await db.$queryRawUnsafe<Row[]>(`

   118→      fts.logContentId,

   120→      fts.sessionId,

content.text: FTS5's `bm25()` accepts per-column weights. The indexed columns are `text` (0), `thinking` (1), `toolResultContent` (2), `toolInput` (3). I'll replace the default `rank` with an explicit `bm25(LogContentFts, 10, 10, 1, 1)`.

input.old_string:
SELECT
        rowid                                              AS logContentId,
        logEventId,
        sessionId,
        timestamp,
        contentType,
        toolName,
        snippet(LogContentFts, -1, '«', '»', '…', 24)   AS snippet,
        rank
      FROM LogContentFts
      WHERE LogContentFts MATCH ?
      ORDER BY rank
      LIMIT ?
input.new_string:
SELECT
        rowid                                              AS logContentId,
        logEventId,
        sessionId,
        timestamp,
        contentType,
        toolName,
        snippet(LogContentFts, -1, '«', '»', '…', 24)   AS snippet,
        -- Weight text/thinking 10× higher than toolResultContent/toolInput.
        bm25(LogContentFts, 10, 10, 1, 1)                AS rank
      FROM LogContentFts
      WHERE LogContentFts MATCH ?
      ORDER BY rank
      LIMIT ?

SELECT
        rowid                                              AS logContentId,
        logEventId,
        sessionId,
        timestamp,
        contentType,
        toolName,
        snippet(LogContentFts, -1, '«', '»', '…', 24)   AS snippet,
        -- Weight text/thinking 10× higher than toolResultContent/toolInput.
        bm25(LogContentFts, 10, 10, 1, 1)                AS rank
      FROM LogContentFts
      WHERE LogContentFts MATCH ?
      ORDER BY rank
      LIMIT ?

content.text: Done. Messages (`text`) and thinking blocks now get 10× the weight of tool results and tool inputs in BM25 scoring, so they'll rank higher when the query matches equally in both.

message.content: Why is the new checkpoint not being indexed?

content.content:
Found 7 files
src/search.ts
src/server.ts
src/indexer.ts
src/handler.ts
src/gitUtils.ts
src/db.ts
src/index.ts

content.text:
Found it. Line 763 does `reset --hard HEAD` which just resets to the current HEAD — it never fetches new commits from the remote. The worktree stays frozen at whatever was checked out at startup.

The fix is to fetch before resetting:

content.content: The file /Users/stephanostsoucas/gossamer/src/server.ts has been updated successfully.

lines: -        // Pull latest commits into the worktree working tree
+        // Fetch latest commits and advance the worktree to the branch tip
const { checkpoints } = await indexAllCheckpointsV2(db, WORKTREE_PATH, undefined, repoDir);

content.text: The `reset --hard HEAD` was a no-op — HEAD never moved because nothing was fetching. Now each poll cycle fetches the branch tip from the remote before indexing, so new checkpoints will be picked up within 30 seconds of being pushed.

message.content: Could this also have to do with the source of truth being the jsonl files?

content.content:
482→export async function indexFullJsonl(
   483→  db: PrismaClient,
   484→  transcriptPath: string,
   485→  sessionLinkId?: number,
   486→): Promise<{ events: number }> {
   487→  const content = readFileSync(transcriptPath, "utf8");
   488→  const events = parseFullJsonl(content);
   489→
   490→  // Pre-load known uuids / messageIds to skip duplicates without per-row queries.
   491→  const [existingUuids, existingMessageIds] = await Promise.all([
   492→    db.logEvent.findMany({ where: { uuid: { not: null } }, select: { uuid: true } })
   493→      .then((rows) => new Set(rows.map((r) => r.uuid as string))),
   494→    db.logEvent.findMany({ where: { messageId: { not: null } }, select: { messageId: true } })
   495→      .then((rows) => new Set(rows.map((r) => r.messageId as string))),
   496→  ]);
   497→
   498→  let count = 0;
   499→
   500→  for (const event of events) {
   501→    // Deduplication: skip if this event was already indexed.
   502→    if (event.uuid && existingUuids.has(event.uuid)) continue;
   503→    if (event.messageId && existingMessageIds.has(event.messageId)) continue;
   504→
   505→    const msg = event.message as Record<string, unknown> | undefined;
   506→
   507→    const logEvent = await db.logEvent.create({
   508→      data: {
   509→        uuid:            event.uuid            ?? null,
   510→        messageId:       event.messageId       ?? null,
   511→        sessionId:       event.sessionId       ?? null,
   512→        parentUuid:      event.parentUuid      ?? null,

   526→      },
   527→      select: { id: true },
   528→    });

   532→      const role = (msg.role as string | undefined) ?? event.type;
   533→      const rawContent = msg.content;

   536→          ? (rawContent as Record<string, unknown>[])
   537→          : typeof rawContent === "string"
   538→          ? [{ type: "text", text: rawContent }]
   539→          : [];
   540→
   541→      for (let i = 0; i < contentArray.length; i++) {

content.text:
Yes, there's a related bug. Look at line 256 — `db.sessionLink.create` (not upsert), combined with the dedup logic in `indexFullJsonl`:

**On every 30s poll cycle, for every already-indexed checkpoint:**
1. A new `SessionLink` record is created pointing to the same `full.jsonl`
2. `indexFullJsonl` runs again — events with `uuid`/`messageId` are skipped by the dedup sets, so no duplicate content rows
3. But events that have **neither** `uuid` nor `messageId` get re-inserted every cycle

There's also a correctness gap: if a checkpoint's `full.jsonl` grows after first indexing (e.g. still in progress), new events at the tail ARE picked up by the dedup logic (new uuids pass the skip check) — that part works. But the duplicate `SessionLink` creation is wasteful and will grow unboundedly.

The fix is to use the `contentHash` as a skip signal — if a `SessionLink` already exists with the same `checkpointMetadataId` + session dir + `contentHash`, skip re-indexing it entirely:

content.content:
71→  sessions         SessionLink[]
    72→  filesTouched     CheckpointMetadataFilePath[]
    73→}
    74→
    75→model SessionLink {
    76→  id                   Int                 @id @default(autoincrement())
    77→  checkpointMetadataId Int?
    78→  checkpointMetadata   CheckpointMetadata? @relation(fields: [checkpointMetadataId], references: [id])
    79→  metadata             String?
    80→  transcript           String?
    81→  context              String?
    82→  contentHash          String?
    83→  prompt               String?
    84→  logEvents            LogEvent[]
    85→
    86→  @@index([checkpointMetadataId])
    87→}
    88→
    89→model CheckpointMetadataFilePath {
    90→  checkpointMetadataId Int
    91→  filePathId           Int
    92→  checkpointMetadata   CheckpointMetadata @relation(fields: [checkpointMetadataId], references: [id])
    93→  filePath             FilePath           @relation("CheckpointMetadataFilePaths", fields: [filePathId], references: [id])
    94→
    95→  @@id([checkpointMetadataId, filePathId])

file.content:
sessions         SessionLink[]
  filesTouched     CheckpointMetadataFilePath[]
}

model SessionLink {
  id                   Int                 @id @default(autoincrement())
  checkpointMetadataId Int?
  checkpointMetadata   CheckpointMetadata? @relation(fields: [checkpointMetadataId], references: [id])
  metadata             String?
  transcript           String?
  context              String?
  contentHash          String?
  prompt               String?
  logEvents            LogEvent[]

  @@index([checkpointMetadataId])
}

model CheckpointMetadataFilePath {
  checkpointMetadataId Int
  filePathId           Int
  checkpointMetadata   CheckpointMetadata @relation(fields: [checkpointMetadataId], references: [id])
  filePath             FilePath           @relation("CheckpointMetadataFilePaths", fields: [filePathId], references: [id])

  @@id([checkpointMetadataId, filePathId])

content.text: No unique constraint on `SessionLink`, so I need to look up by `(checkpointMetadataId, transcript)` and use `contentHash` to decide whether to skip or re-index. I'll implement that without needing a schema change:

content.content: The file /Users/stephanostsoucas/gossamer/src/indexer.ts has been updated successfully.

lines: // Read actual hash content (not the file path)
+    // Look up an existing SessionLink for this transcript path.
+    // If the content hash hasn't changed, this session is fully indexed — skip it.
+      // Hash changed (JSONL grew) — update the stored hash and re-index.

content.text:
Now each poll cycle:
- **Same hash** → skip entirely (already fully indexed)
- **Changed hash** (JSONL grew mid-session) → update the record and re-run `indexFullJsonl`, which skips already-seen events by uuid/messageId and picks up new ones at the tail
- **No existing link** → create and index as before

So yes, the JSONL files were indirectly the problem — the code was re-creating `SessionLink` rows and re-scanning transcripts on every poll, which masked whether indexing was actually doing anything useful and made the duplicate-SessionLink issue hard to notice.

content.text:
The server log tells the story — many checkpoints (including `c100d245d01e`) exist as directory stubs with no `metadata.json`. That's the old `reset --hard HEAD` problem: the worktree is frozen at a stale commit, so the fetch gets the directory tree but not the file contents.

My fix in `server.ts` (fetch → `reset --hard FETCH_HEAD`) will unblock this, but the server is running the old compiled binary. It needs to be rebuilt and restarted to pick it up. Let me verify the build compiles clean first:

content.content:
> claude-hooks-listener@1.0.0 build
> prisma generate && tsc

Loaded Prisma config from prisma.config.ts.

Prisma schema loaded from prisma/schema.prisma.

✔ Generated Prisma Client (v7.4.2) to ./prisma/generated/client in 127ms

Start by importing your Prisma Client (See: https://pris.ly/d/importing-client)

toolUseResult.stdout:
> claude-hooks-listener@1.0.0 build
> prisma generate && tsc

Loaded Prisma config from prisma.config.ts.

Prisma schema loaded from prisma/schema.prisma.

✔ Generated Prisma Client (v7.4.2) to ./prisma/generated/client in 127ms

Start by importing your Prisma Client (See: https://pris.ly/d/importing-client)

content.text:
Clean. Now restart the server — you'll need to kill the current one and start a new one:

```bash
pkill -f "claude-hook-handler serve" && claude-hook-handler serve --db ~/.claude/hook-handler.db --port 3000 --repo-dir ~/gossamer
```

Once restarted, the first poll cycle will run `git fetch origin entire/checkpoints/v1` followed by `reset --hard FETCH_HEAD`, which will populate all those empty checkpoint dirs with their actual files. The `c15bd67e` session will appear once `entire` commits a checkpoint for it to the branch — the indexer can't see it before that since the JSONL files only live in `~/.claude/projects/` until then.
